retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Both environmental and genetic factors contribute to the development and progression of MASLD. First-degree relatives of patients with MASLD are at higher risk than the general population.

retrieved_docs.title: Mallory Bodies -- Clinicopathologic Correlations -- Liver Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Among individuals who consume alcohol, ubiquitin immunostaining offers a sensitive and specific method to detect MDBs. These inclusions appear not only in the liver but also in muscle and neural tissues, supporting the theory of a shared intracellular pathway for protein synthesis, degradation, and stress response. MDBs may act as a transient protective response to cellular injury or as a step toward progressive hepatic damage. In animal models, MDBs reappear within 2 to 3 days after alcohol cessation, indicating a rapid recurrence linked to prior exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: Abetalipoproteinemia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic cholestatic liver disease Combined neuropathy and ataxia Familial vitamin E deficiency Hereditary sensorimotor neuropathies· Retinal degeneration Secondary cancers Spinocerebellar disorders

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Adenoma -- Etiology -- Hepatocyte Nuclear Factor -1α Inactivated Mutations
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with hepatocyte nuclear factor-1α (HNF-1α) inactivated mutations comprise 35% to 40% of cases. The condition involves biallelic mutations of the T-cell factor-1 gene that encodes the transcription factor HNF-1α. This protein is involved in hepatocyte differentiation, liver development, and glucose and lipid metabolism. HCA lesions in this group are noticeably more steatotic on biopsy. Women are mostly affected. The mutation is likewise often associated with mature-onset diabetes of the young (MODY3). About 90% of the mutations are somatic, although MODY3 association can involve the germline and commonly manifests with adenomatosis. Lesion size smaller than 5 cm reduces complication risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Abetalipoproteinemia -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic scan or ultrasonography to assess changes of fatty liver Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spinocerebellar region may show degeneration Eye and retinal examination and imaging to check for retinal damage

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Causes of Obesity and Comprehensive Diagnostic Evaluation -- Function -- Lipodystrophy
retrieved_docs.content: Lipodystrophy syndromes encompass a group of rare disorders characterized by partial or complete loss of subcutaneous adipose tissue. This adipose tissue deficiency leads to abnormal fat redistribution and ectopic fat deposition, often resulting in severe metabolic complications, including insulin resistance, hypertriglyceridemia, and hepatic steatosis. Prompt recognition of these syndromes remains essential for initiating appropriate treatment and providing genetic counseling, especially in congenital forms. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Evaluation -- Assessment of genetic risk factors
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians in specialized centers may consider genetic risk profiling, including assessment of the PNPLA3 p.I148M variant and polygenic risk scores, to enhance personalized risk stratification and facilitate disease sub-phenotyping in patients with MASLD. Genetic risk variants serve as valuable tools for clinical studies examining disease progression patterns. Individuals presenting with strong family histories of severe hepatic disease in first-degree relatives or early-onset severe phenotypes, particularly in the absence of conventional metabolic triggers or in patients with normal body weight, warrant consideration for comprehensive genetic evaluation through next-generation sequencing approaches to identify coexisting treatable genetic causes of liver disease. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Diabetic Hypoglycemia -- Evaluation -- Hypoglycemia Secondary to IEM
retrieved_docs.content: Inborn errors of metabolism are rare causes of hypoglycemia. Diagnostic workup is described as below, and further testing should be conducted if suspected. [20] Glycogen storage disease: fasting hypoglycemia with hepatomegaly, which can be investigated with a glucose tolerance test with lactate and ketones measurement. Glucokinase activating mutation: postprandial hypoglycemia, which can be investigated with a mixed meal test. Monocarboxylate 1 mutation (MCT1): autosomal dominant associated with exercise-induced hypoglycemia Fatty acid oxidation defect: fasting hypoglycemia with raised cyclic phosphokinase and free fatty acid levels Gluconeogenesis disorder: fasting hypoglycemia with raised alanine, glycerol, lactate, and ketones.

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Day and James proposed a 2-hit model of MASLD pathogenesis in 1998. The first hit is caused by insulin resistance, which leads to the accumulation of fat droplets, specifically triglycerides, in the cytoplasm of hepatocytes, resulting in the development of steatosis. Insulin resistance causes an excess delivery of free fatty acids and triglycerides to the liver, resulting in decreased excretion and accumulation. Additionally, excess carbohydrates serve as a stimulus for de novo fatty acid synthesis in the liver.

retrieved_docs.title: Mallory Bodies -- Clinical Significance -- Areas for Further Research
retrieved_docs.content: Autophagy plays a role in the degradation of MDBs, alongside other intracellular clearance mechanisms. [25] [26] Fenofibrate, a lipid-lowering fibric acid derivative, has been shown to prevent MDB formation by preserving intermediate filament integrity and limiting cytoskeletal disruption. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Introduction -- Types of Steatotic Liver Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Excess adiposity Body-mass index (BMI): A BMI of ≥25 kg/m² in the general population or  ≥23 kg/m² in individuals of Asian ancestry. Central (visceral) obesity (waist circumference thresholds): • Waist circumference of  ≥94 cm in men and  ≥80 cm in women of European ancestry • Waist circumference of  ≥90 cm in men and  ≥80 cm in women of South-Asian or Chinese ancestry • Waist circumference of  ≥85 cm in men and  ≥90 cm in women of Japanese ancestry Disorders of glucose metabolism Prediabetes: Glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c) 5.7% to 6.4 %; fasting plasma glucose ranging from 100 to 125 mg/dL Type 2 diabetes mellitus: HbA1c of  ≥6.5%; fasting plasma glucose of  ≥126 mg/dL Hypertriglyceridemia: Plasma triglycerides ≥150 mg/dL or treatment with lipid-lowering agents High-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol: <40 mg/dL in men or ≤50 mg/dL in women Arterial hypertension : Resting blood pressure of 130/85 mm Hg or greater or ongoing antihypertensive treatment. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Treatment / Management -- Alcohol avoidance
retrieved_docs.content: Abstinence from alcohol is recommended for all patients with MASLD.

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Transplantation -- Indications -- Metabolic liver diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Familial amyloid polyneuropathy LT has proven effective in familial amyloid polyneuropathy due to a mutation in the transthyretin gene. LT is most effective in patients younger than 50 because LT neither improves nor reverses neurological symptoms, but rather prevents disease progression. [46] Also, patients with hereditary renal amyloidosis inherited as an autosomal dominant gene usually benefit from liver and renal transplantation. LT doesn't seem to affect the ocular or cardiac effects of amyloidosis. [51]

Consequently, clinicians should consider the lipid‑lowering benefit of Klofibrát while avoiding its use in individuals with hepatic steatosis to prevent potential liver‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 724
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates are a class of plain lipid‑modifying drugs that include the agents bezafibrát, klofibrát (clofibrate) and fenofibrát (fenofibrate). All three drugs are members of the fibrate family and act to modify plasma lipid levels. Clofibrate exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing lipolysis, which contributes to its lipid‑lowering activity. However, clofibrate is contraindicated in patients with تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic congestion), indicating that use of this fibrate should be avoided in the presence of liver congestion. The condition تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ (alcoholic congestion) is a specific form of hepatic congestion, as it is described as having تَشَمُّع كَبِدي as a child concept. Clinically, alcoholic congestion is associated with فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم (hyperkalemia), highlighting the need to monitor serum potassium levels in affected patients. Together, these relationships clarify the classification of fibrate drugs, the metabolic effect of clofibrate, and important safety considerations related to liver congestion and its alcoholic variant.

Juvenile nephropathic cystinosis, a rare inherited disorder classified under “other nutritional, endocrine, and metabolic disorders,” is related to amino‑acid metabolism disorders, inversely related to broader metabolic disorders, and exemplifies the connection between genetic metabolic defects and renal disease (cystinosis of the kidney). Carbohydrate‑metabolism disorders are a subset of metabolic disorders and intersect with other hormonal disorders, highlighting the integrated network of nutrient‑metabolism, renal, and endocrine dysfunctions that must be evaluated comprehensively in patient care. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 370
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a class of lipid‑modifying agents that includes the specific fibrates bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát. Within this class, klofibrát is noted to produce the physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, reflecting its mechanism of reducing the breakdown of triglycerides. Additionally, klofibrát carries a contraindication in the setting of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic impairment), indicating that patients with liver dysfunction should not receive this medication. These interconnections highlight both the categorical relationship of individual fibrates to their broader drug class and the important therapeutic effect and safety profile associated with klofibrát.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism encompass conditions such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia). Within this broader category, فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is a specific manifestation that is mapped to the class of lipid‑metabolism disorders. Type IIa hyperlipidemia follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that Autosomal dominant inheritance is the genetic transmission mechanism for this particular disorder. Recognizing that فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is a form of فرط البروتينات الشحمية and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians diagnose, screen families, and manage patients with lipid metabolism abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2745
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are classified as a type of metabolic disorder, indicating that abnormalities in lipid processing fall under the broader category of metabolic disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1763
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that include specific entities such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية, which is recognized as a particular type of lipid metabolism disorder. Within this same category, the condition ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) is listed as a member, indicating that early‑onset diabetes is considered part of the spectrum of lipid metabolism abnormalities. The SMQ is further characterized by the clinical effect تأثير إدرار البول, reflecting the polyuric manifestation commonly seen in new‑onset hyperglycemia. Additionally, the disorder فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is mapped to the disorders of lipid metabolism group and is known to follow an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, highlighting a genetic basis for this specific hyperlipidemic condition. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid metabolism disorders can intersect with early diabetes presentations and hereditary factors, underscoring the importance of integrated assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism constitute a broad category of metabolic conditions affecting the processing and regulation of fats in the body, and hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) is a specific disorder within this category. Hyperlipoproteinemia is characterized by abnormally high levels of lipoproteins in the blood, reflecting a particular manifestation of the underlying disturbances in lipid metabolism. Recognizing hyperlipoproteinemia as a subset of disorders of lipid metabolism underscores its role in the spectrum of lipid‑related diseases and informs targeted diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1439
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic disturbances that encompass specific conditions such as اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي and فرط البروتينات الشحمية. This category also includes the clinical concept ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) as one of its members, indicating a link between lipid abnormalities and early‑onset hyperglycemia. In classification systems, disorders of lipid metabolism are mapped to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ, a form of familial hyperlipidemia. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that the trait is transmitted through a single copy of the altered gene from an affected parent. Together, these relationships highlight how lipid metabolism disorders are interconnected with other metabolic disorders, specific disease entities, and genetic inheritance mechanisms, informing both diagnostic categorization and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the network of relationships portrays diabetes as a multifaceted condition that influences and is influenced by genetic abnormalities, neonatal presentations, limb and ocular complications, metabolic comorbidities, diagnostic testing, and medication safety, all of which are essential considerations for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1052
retrieved_docs.content: Nemoc is associated with cases of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance disorders, indicating that patients with Nemoc frequently exhibit disturbances in hydration status, electrolyte concentrations, and systemic pH regulation. Similarly, alcoholic hepatitis (الْتِهابُ الكَبِدِ الكحوليّ) is clinically associated with the same spectrum of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance disorders, reflecting that hepatic inflammation due to alcohol often leads to comparable metabolic derangements. In addition, Nemc is associated with an amino acid metabolism disorder (اضطراب استقلاب الأحماض الأمينية), suggesting that this condition predisposes individuals to abnormalities in the processing and utilization of amino acids. Together, these relationships highlight that both Nemoc and alcoholic hepatitis share a common clinical link to disturbances in fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base homeostasis, while Nemc contributes a distinct metabolic complication involving amino acid metabolism. Recognizing these interconnected pathophysiological patterns can aid clinicians in anticipating and managing the overlapping and separate metabolic challenges presented by these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 127
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a therapeutic class of lipid‑lowering agents that includes the individual drugs bezafibrát, klofibrát, fenofibrát and gemfibrozil. Each of these agents is classified as a member of the fibrate group, reflecting their shared mechanism of reducing plasma triglycerides and modulating lipoprotein metabolism. Among them, klofibrát exerts a physiologic effect characterized by decreased lipolysis, which contributes to its lipid‑lowering action. However, klofibrát is contraindicated in the presence of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic congestion), indicating that patients with this hepatic condition should avoid its use. Gemfibrozil is further linked to the formulation category G [Preparations], of which it is a parent compound, underscoring its inclusion in a specific preparation subgroup. Collectively, these relationships delineate the classification, pharmacologic effects, and safety considerations pertinent to fibrate therapy in patient care.
